hook: You already know how to multiply matrices and find eigenvalues, but have you ever wondered why symmetric matrices always have real eigenvalues and perpendicular eigenvectors? This unit reveals that the inner product structure on a vector space imposes powerful constraints on which linear operators can exist and how they must behave. The payoff is stunning: nearly every matrix decomposition used in modern data science, physics, and engineering flows from the elegant theory you are about to build.
concept: The unifying idea is that pairing a linear operator with an inner product forces deep geometric structure — eigenvectors become orthogonal, spectra become real, and every operator admits a clean diagonal-like decomposition. Formally, this is the study of *self-adjoint, normal, and positive operators on inner product spaces* and their spectral properties.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to construct the adjoint $T^*$ of a linear operator $T$ and understand it as the unique operator satisfying $\langle Tv, w \rangle = \langle v, T^*w \rangle$ for all vectors $v$ and $w$.
Distinguish the key classes of operators — self-adjoint ($T = T^*$) and normal ($TT^* = T^*T$) — and identify the algebraic and geometric properties that make them so special.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: The Spectral Theorem for Real Symmetric Matrices (H)
topic_roadmap.description: Prove and apply the real spectral theorem: every real symmetric matrix is orthogonally diagonalizable with real eigenvalues and mutually orthogonal eigenvectors.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: The Spectral Theorem for Normal Operators (H)
topic_roadmap.description: Extend the spectral theorem to normal operators on complex inner product spaces, showing that any normal operator admits an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors.
Master the characterization of positive (semi)definite operators via the condition $\langle Tv, v \rangle > 0$, and connect positivity to eigenvalue sign and the existence of square roots $\sqrt{T}$.
Apply the SVD to decompose any matrix $A$ as $A = U\Sigma V^*$, unlocking a universal geometric interpretation of linear maps and a foundation for dimensionality reduction and least-squares problems.
